PROJECT CODEX

AI-Native Code Coordination Architecture

A whitepaper on replacing filesystem + Git + PR review

for the age of concurrent AI agent development

Nexum Corpus

Working Document — v1.0

March 2026

## Table of Contents

## 1. Core Thesis

As software development scales from sequential human teams to fleets of concurrent AI agents, the
foundational twenty-year-old paradigm of Filesystem + Git + PR review becomes a critical bottleneck.
The industry’s knee-jerk proposal to “store code in a database like Postgres” fundamentally
misunderstands the problem.

CENTRAL CLAIM

The transition to machine-speed development is not a storage problem. It is a distributed semantic
consensus problem.

To enable an AI coding entity to ceaselessly produce without collapsing into architectural rot, the
system must separate the probabilistic generation engine (the AI/LLM) from a deterministic
coordination and validation chassis. The AI reads and computes. The architecture writes and governs.

```
This separation is not merely architectural preference. Every production AI coding system in 2025—2026—OpenAI Codex, Augment Code, Devin—avoids concurrent code editing entirely, defaulting to isolated git worktrees or branches merged after the fact. This document proposes the infrastructure required to move beyond isolation into true concurrent semantic coordination.
```

## 2. The 5-Layer Blueprint

The architecture comprises five layers, each replacing a specific bottleneck in the current
paradigm. Layers are ordered by criticality: Layer 1 is the foundation everything depends on; Layer
5 is the adoption vehicle that makes the rest invisible to existing toolchains.

### Layer 1: Semantic Code Graph (The Source of Truth)

Replacing flat files and naive database rows with a purpose-built store that represents code as
lossless Concrete Syntax Trees (CSTs) with mapped dependency graphs.

#### Concept

Nodes are semantic units—functions, classes, interfaces, modules. Edges are relationships—calls,
inherits, implements, imports. Every piece of source text, including whitespace, comments, and
formatting, is preserved in the tree. The graph is the single source of truth; files are a rendered
view.

#### Mechanism

Granular, semantic-level locking. Agent A and Agent B can edit different functions within the same
traditional “file” simultaneously without conflict, because the graph tracks ownership at the node
level, not the file level.

#### RAG Integration

The graph becomes the ultimate deterministic index for Advanced RAG, allowing the AI to query exact
architectural impacts before generating intent. A 2025 study (arXiv 2601.08773) found that
deterministic AST-derived graph RAG built its ontology in 2.81 seconds versus 200 seconds for LLM-
based graph extraction, achieved perfect accuracy on test queries, and cost 10x less. The Joern
project’s Code Property Graph—combining AST, control flow, and program dependence graphs—is the
established industry standard for this pattern.

#### Prior Art and Tool Landscape

The lossless roundtrip problem—parsing code into a tree and reconstructing it byte-for-byte—is
solved per-language but not universally:

Tool

Function

Status

Key Limitation

Roslyn (RedGreen)

Lossless CST for C#/VB

Production

C#/VB only

Rowan / cstree

Language-agnostic lossless CST (Rust)

Active

Needs parser integration per language

Tree-sitter

Multi-language parsing (200+ grammars)

Active

Not self-contained lossless; grammar quality varies

LibCST

Lossless CST for Python

Active

Python only

SwiftSyntax

Lossless CST for Swift

Active

Swift only

Difftastic

Structural AST-aware diffing

Active

Read-only; does not address merging

Mergiraf

```
AST-aware git merge driver
```

Active

Moving away from tree-sitter; GPLv3

ast-grep

Structural code search/rewrite

Active

Rewrite is text-based, not tree transformation

SemanticMerge

Language-specific semantic merge

Discontinued

Bundled into Unity VCS; C#/Java/VB only

Recommended architecture: Rowan (Rust) as the CST library, tree-sitter as the parsing frontend, with
a per-language trivia assignment layer that converts tree-sitter’s byte-gap whitespace model into
explicit trivia nodes attached to tokens (mirroring Roslyn’s proven approach).

#### Graph Database Selection

Sub-millisecond AST traversal requires an embedded, in-process engine—network round-trips in client-
server architectures add 1–10ms+ overhead. The tiered recommendation:

- Hot path (per-agent): petgraph (Rust) or CozoDB embedded. Nanosecond-level traversals, zero serialization. CozoDB adds Datalog queries, MVCC concurrency, and sub-1ms two-hop traversals with persistence.
- Warm path (cross-agent sync): Memgraph (C++). 1.09ms one-hop expansion, 32,028 QPS—roughly 114x faster throughput than Neo4j on equivalent benchmarks.
- Rich modeling (optional): TypeDB 3 (Rust rewrite, Dec 2024). Best type system for semantic code modeling but 8–60ms latency disqualifies it from the hot path.

#### Open Research Vectors

- Universal trivia assignment: Per-language logic converting tree-sitter’s byte gaps into Roslyn-style explicit trivia. Must be built for each target language.
- Comment association during transformation: Comments float between AST nodes without syntactic homes. Reassignment during code moves is inherently ambiguous—cited as the single biggest unsolved problem by multiple compiler teams.
- Cross-language embedded syntax: HTML+JS+CSS, JSX, and template literals have partial tree-sitter support but no lossless roundtrip across language boundaries.

### Layer 2: Intent-Based Coordination (The Workspace)

```
Replacing isolated git branches with a shared workspace governed by declared intentions. This layer contains the architecture’s hardest unsolved problem.
```

#### Concept

Agents do not write code directly. They publish an Intent Payload (e.g., “Refactoring auth.ts to
support OAuth2”). The coordination layer analyzes the Semantic Graph to check for lock collisions or
semantic conflicts. If a collision is detected, the intent is queued or rejected before compute is
wasted generating the code.

#### The Core Problem: Structural Validity Is Not Semantic Validity

Existing tree CRDTs guarantee that concurrent edits produce a structurally valid tree. They do not
guarantee the tree represents a semantically valid program. Two agents both adding a parameter to a
function call could produce a structurally valid tree with incorrect arity. No existing CRDT
addresses this.

THE GAP

Conflict-free means there is no conflict in the data structure semantics, not that concurrent
operations on a syntax tree produce a valid program. This distinction is the central unsolved
problem.

#### Prior Art: Tree CRDTs

- Kleppmann et al. (IEEE TPDS 2021): “A Highly-Available Move Operation for Replicated Trees.” Formally verified (Isabelle/HOL proofs). Solves concurrent move operations that could create cycles. ~5,500 ops/sec CRDT mode, ~22,000 ops/sec with leader. Demonstrated bugs in both Google Drive and Dropbox.
- Loro (Rust + JS): Most production-ready tree CRDT implementation. Incorporates Kleppmann’s algorithm with native movable trees, rich text (Fugue algorithm), lists, and maps.
- Yjs / Automerge: Excellent for text collaboration but limited tree support. No native move operations or tree-specific conflict resolution.
- Martin et al. (2012, arXiv:1201.1784): Foundational abstract unordered and ordered tree CRDT—theoretical basis without move support.
- Sinchuk et al. (2016, JetBrains): Verified Operational Transformation for trees, proven in Coq.

#### Prior Art: Saga Pattern for Agent Coordination

The saga pattern (Garcia-Molina & Salem, 1987) maps naturally to agent code mutations: declare
intent, acquire semantic locks on affected AST regions, execute mutations as local operations,
commit on success or execute compensating transactions on conflict. Production implementations exist
in Temporal.io, Azure Durable Functions, and AWS Step Functions.

#### What Production Systems Actually Do

Every production AI coding system avoids concurrent AST editing:

- Augment Code “Intent”: Coordinator decomposes specs, implementors execute in isolated git worktrees, verifiers validate. No concurrent AST editing.
- OpenAI Codex multi-agent: Per-agent git worktrees with post-hoc merge.
- Devin 2.0: Parallel sub-tasks on separate branches.

No production system performs concurrent AST-level editing. All use isolation + merge.

#### Proposed Mechanism

A layered coordination approach combining five mechanisms:

- Kleppmann-style tree CRDT (Loro) for structural convergence guarantees.
- Semantic validation layer that rejects structurally-valid but semantically-invalid merges (post-merge type checking).
- Intent declaration protocol where agents announce planned AST regions before editing.
- Semantic locking at function/class granularity based on commutativity analysis (two agents adding independent methods = concurrent; two agents modifying same method body = serialized).
- Saga-pattern compensating transactions for rollback when semantic validation fails.

#### Open Research Vectors

- Semantic CRDT validation: Ensuring merged ASTs produce type-correct programs. The single hardest open problem in the entire architecture.
- Intent pre-validation protocol: No working implementation exists anywhere for agents announcing planned AST modifications and a system checking compatibility before execution.
- Compensating transaction design: Reverting a function rename requires restoring the original name and all call sites—the combinatorial complexity of “undo” in a semantic graph.

### Layer 3: Continuous Automated Validation (The Safety Net)

Replacing batch human PR reviews with real-time, rule-based execution. Unbounded autonomy leads to
entropy—every semantic mutation triggers an immediate, deterministic validation pipeline.

#### Mechanism

Strict, programmatic enforcement running in milliseconds: type checking (Pyright, tsc), bounded test
execution, and architectural constraint checking. Human oversight is exception-based—humans are
pinged only when validation fails on a risk-flagged boundary (database schema change, public API
alteration).

#### Prior Art: Incremental Computation

- Salsa (Rust, 3.5M+ downloads): Models computation as a DAG of pure functions with automatic dependency tracking, memoization, and early-cutoff optimization. If a derived value’s result hasn’t changed despite input changes, downstream dependents skip recomputation. Powers rust-analyzer.
- rust-analyzer’s durability system: Categorizes queries by volatility, reducing no-op re-analysis from ~300ms to near-instant. Key invariant directly applicable to Codex: “Typing inside a function body never invalidates global derived data.”
- Buck2 (Meta): DICE incremental dependency graph with remote-execution-first architecture. Compatible with Bazel’s RE API.
- Turborepo: Content-aware hashing where build artifacts are cached by source hash and shared across team/CI via remote cache.

#### Open Research Vectors

- Function-level incremental test execution: Current selective testing operates at module/file granularity. No system isolates and tests individual semantic units based on AST dependency analysis.
- Cross-language real-time validation: rust-analyzer achieves sub-second semantic validation for Rust alone. No production system does this across multiple languages during editing.

### Layer 4: Immutable Event Log (The Nervous System)

```
Replacing git history with richer, queryable provenance. Every approved mutation is an event in an append-only log, enabling complete auditability, replay capability, and semantic rollback.
```

#### Concept

The log bypasses the concept of “snapshots.” Instead of “revert the last 5 commits,” an operator
says “undo the OAuth feature”—the system identifies all mutations associated with that semantic
intent and generates compensating events. The event stream is embedded for RAG, allowing agents to
search the causal chain of why a function was altered historically.

#### Stream Infrastructure Options

NATS JetStream

Redpanda

Kafka

Language

Go (single binary)

C++

JVM (Java/Scala)

Throughput

Up to 800K msgs/sec

Kafka-compatible

Highest at scale

P99 Latency

Low

8–15ms

15–25ms

Ops Complexity

Minimal

Moderate

High (ZooKeeper/KRaft)

Best For

Simplest deployment

Low tail latency

Max ecosystem maturity

Recommendation: NATS JetStream for initial implementation—single binary, built-in KV store, natural
event-sourcing semantics. Migrate to Redpanda or Kafka only if throughput requirements exceed NATS
capabilities at scale.

#### Open Research Vectors

- Semantic event sourcing for code: Genuinely novel. Event sourcing has never been applied to semantic code changes in any mainstream project. Git is append-only but operates at text-diff level.
- State rehydration: Optimizing speed of rebuilding the Layer 1 Semantic Graph from the event log. Datomic’s immutable fact-with-time model is the closest conceptual analog.

### Layer 5: Compatibility Shim (The Trojan Horse)

Replacing the need to rewrite the entire developer tooling ecosystem to achieve adoption. Humans and
legacy CI/CD pipelines must still interact with the codebase normally.

#### Mechanism

A custom Language Server Protocol (LSP) proxy. Human IDEs think they are talking to a standard
filesystem. The LSP translates human text edits into AST mutations and feeds agent intents into the
IDE as inline visual diagnostics. LSP is deliberately extensible via JSON-RPC custom methods—custom
methods prefixed with a namespace (e.g., codex/semanticDiff, codex/validationStatus) extend the
protocol without breaking standard editor compatibility.

Production LSP proxies already exist: Val Town’s vtlsp supports arbitrary middleware for
request/response transformation, and lsp-proxy (techee) merges diagnostics from multiple language
servers. All major AI coding tools—Cursor, Continue.dev, OpenCode—augment LSP rather than replace
it, validating the shim approach.

#### Open Research Vectors

- LSP translation fidelity: Mapping standard textDocument/didChange events into deterministic AST mutation intents in real-time without perceptible latency.

## 3. Implementation Reality Check

The separation between AI and architecture is not a philosophical preference—it is the mechanism
that prevents entropy:

- Read-Path (AI/RAG): The AI uses GraphRAG to read Layer 1 and Layer 4, synthesizing context to understand system state.
- Compute-Path (AI/LLM): The AI generates an intent and the corresponding AST mutations.
- Write-Path (Deterministic Architecture): Layers 2, 3, and 4 validate, test, and commit the mutation. The AI does not govern the write-path.

DESIGN PRINCIPLE

The AI proposes. The architecture disposes. No probabilistic system governs the write-path.

## 4. Beyond-Git Prior Art

Three existing systems embody fragments of the Codex philosophy, each offering a distinct design
lesson:

#### Unison: Content-Addressed Code

Every definition is hashed (512-bit SHA3) based on its AST with named arguments replaced by
positional references and all dependencies replaced by their hashes. Names become separately stored
metadata—renaming is instantaneous and free. Merges avoid formatting conflicts entirely because code
is stored as ASTs, not text. The limitation: deeply integrated into Unison’s functional, statically-
typed design. The principles—hashing ASTs, separating names from identity, append-only code
databases—are directly adoptable.

#### Pijul: Category-Theoretic Patches

Based on Mimram and di Giusto’s theory where version control is about pushouts, Pijul ensures
independent patches commute: applying them in any order produces identical results with the same
version identifier. This property eliminates an entire class of coordination problems for parallel
agent execution. Pijul solved Darcs’ exponential merge complexity by representing file state as
directed graphs of byte chunks. Alpha-stage with a tiny ecosystem, but the mathematical foundation
is directly relevant.

#### Jujutsu (jj): Conflicts as Data

Started as a Google 20% project. Introduces conflicts as committable data rather than blocking
markers. Agents could proceed with work even in the presence of conflicts, resolving them later—a
dramatically better model for parallel execution than Git’s “stop and fix” approach. Its operation
log enables full undo support, and automatic rebase propagates conflict resolutions through
descendants.

#### Emerging Projects

- Aura (Apache 2.0): AST Merkle-graph on top of Git with function-level revert and a gatekeeper hook blocking commits when intent doesn’t match AST modifications. Claims 80–93% reduction in LLM context for agent handoffs.
- sem (Ataraxy Labs): Entity-level diffs (“function validateToken was added”) using tree-sitter with structural hashing for rename detection.
- LastMerge (2025): Language-agnostic structured merge across 350+ languages via tree-sitter. Only 15% fewer false positives than language-specific JDime—suggesting universal structured merge is viable.

## 5. Gap Analysis

#### Production-Ready

Per-language lossless CSTs (Roslyn, LibCST, rowan), tree CRDTs with moves (Loro), incremental
computation (Salsa), multi-language parsing (tree-sitter), LSP proxy architecture (vtlsp), event
streaming (NATS/Kafka/Redpanda), structured AST diffing (GumTree, difftastic), graph databases at
various latency tiers.

#### Requires Significant Integration Work

Multi-language lossless CST via rowan + tree-sitter (needs per-language trivia layers), GraphRAG
over code graphs (academic prototypes exist, no production system), semantic locking (database
theory exists, needs adaptation to code ASTs), structured merge across languages (LastMerge/Mergiraf
early but functional).

#### Must Be Invented

Component

Description

Layer

Semantic CRDT validation

Ensuring merged ASTs produce type-correct, semantically valid programs

1–2

Intent pre-validation protocol

Agents announcing planned AST mods; system checking compatibility before execution

2

Semantic event sourcing

Append-only log of semantic mutations with semantic rollback capability

4

Universal trivia assignment

Per-language whitespace/comment-to-token attachment logic

1

Cross-language comment association

Where comments belong after code moves/deletions

1

Function-level incremental testing

Isolating and testing individual semantic units via AST dependency analysis

3

ASSESSMENT

Every layer has strong prior art for its core mechanisms. The genuinely novel contributions—semantic
CRDT validation, intent-based pre-coordination, and semantic event sourcing—sit at Layers 1–2 and 4,
precisely where the hardest engineering and research challenges concentrate.

## 6. Essential Reading

#### Tree CRDTs and Distributed Consensus

- Kleppmann et al., “A Highly-Available Move Operation for Replicated Trees” (IEEE TPDS 2021)
- Martin et al., “Abstract Unordered and Ordered Trees CRDT” (2012, arXiv:1201.1784)
- Sinchuk, Chuprikov, Solomatov, “Verified Operational Transformation for Trees” (2016, JetBrains)
- Weidner & Kleppmann, “Fugue: A Basic List CRDT” (2023)

#### Semantic Code Analysis and Merging

- Sousa et al., “Verifying Semantic Conflict-Freedom in Three-Way Program Merges” (2018)
- Cavalcanti et al., “MolhadoRef: A Refactoring-Aware Software Configuration Management Tool”
- LastMerge, “A Language-Agnostic Structured Tool for Code Integration” (2025, arXiv:2507.19687)
- Schesch et al., ASE 2024 comprehensive evaluation of structured merge tools

#### Incremental Computation and Build Systems

- Salsa algorithm documentation and rust-analyzer architecture guide
- Buck2 engineering blog (Meta, 2023)—DICE incremental dependency graph
- rust-analyzer “Durable Incrementality” (2023)

#### Beyond-Git Architectures

- Unison language documentation: “The Big Idea”
- Pijul theory documentation—category-theoretic patch model
- Jujutsu VCS—conflicts as first-class data, operation log
